serving.description: 1 sandwich portion or salad topping portion
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one sandwich portion or a salad topping portion as served ala carte.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken ham, sliced, cured poultry product (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data for assembled sandwich context
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cured chicken/turkey ham used as primary nutrient anchor (FDC ID 174608 — Turkey and chicken loaf, cured; and FDC ID 172958 — Chicken roll, light meat). Values adapted and cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for local serving context. The input per_100g values (energy 186.67 kcal, protein 12.0 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 18.67 g) are consistent with a mixed chicken ham sandwich assembly including bread and vegetables, and are retained as the best available estimate. Sodium 566.67 mg/100g is consistent with cured processed chicken ham products in USDA SR Legacy. All values treated as medium-confidence estimates for the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous food entries for mixed sandwich with processed meat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken ham ala carte as an assembled dish. GI estimated at 54 (low, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification where 55 or less is low) based on the analogous food 'sandwich with mixed filling on white bread' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The bread component dominates GI; wholemeal bread would lower GI to approximately 45–48. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 28.0) / 100 = 15.1. Carbohydrate per serving = 18.67 g/100g × 150 g / 100 = 28.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 566.67 mg/100g from cured chicken ham; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, representing 37–43% of the 2000–2300 mg daily limit for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to the inherently high sodium content of cured chicken ham; if consumed, request low-sodium chicken ham, avoid additional high-sodium sauces, and restrict portion to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 18.67 g/100g (28.0 g per 150 g serving) with low GI of 54; dietary fibre 2.0 g/100g and high protein 12.0 g/100g provide partial glycaemic modulation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions; request wholemeal bread to lower GI, avoid sugary dressings, and limit to one serving to keep glycaemic load within the low-to-moderate range (GL 15).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g/100g and trans fat 0.07 g/100g are within acceptable limits, but sodium 566.67 mg/100g and processed meat nitrates in chicken ham are cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to the high sodium content and processed meat nitrate exposure; choose low-sodium chicken ham, avoid butter-based spreads, and pair with a vegetable-rich salad base rather than bread.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 566.67 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; processed meat nitrates in chicken ham may have mild pro-inflammatory effects.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request low-sodium chicken ham, tomato-free preparation if nightshade-sensitive, and avoid high-sodium dressings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Ham Ala Carte is a Western-style assembled dish available as a standalone menu item in Singapore food courts and cafes, typically served as a sandwich, open-faced sandwich, or salad topping. The dish centres on sliced chicken ham — a processed, cured poultry product made from chicken breast or thigh meat that has been seasoned, cured with salt and nitrates, and formed into a loaf before slicing. It is commonly paired with bread (white or wholemeal), lettuce, tomato, cucumber, and a condiment such as mayonnaise, mustard, or a vinegar-based dressing. The flavour profile is mildly savoury and salty with a soft, slightly springy texture from the cured meat. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 187 kcal, 12 g protein, 6.7 g fat, and 18.7 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 566.7 mg — elevated due to the curing process. Dietary fibre is 2.0 g/100g and vitamin C is 6.7 mg/100g from vegetable components. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients; the low GI of approximately 54 and medium glycaemic load make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes patients in controlled portions. The dish is popular as a light lunch or breakfast option and is widely available ala carte in Western fast-food stalls, school canteens, and hospital cafeterias across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cured chicken/turkey ham products and are plausible for an assembled sandwich dish; however, no exact approved-source entry for 'Chicken Ham Ala Carte' as a composite dish exists. Values are adapted estimates cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo context. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed-filling sandwiches. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for assembled chicken ham sandwich dish not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this assembled dish not reported in the primary approved sources consulted; estimated ~220 mg/100g noted in input but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this assembled dish not available in approved sources at the required specificity. All core macros (protein_g 12.0, fat_g 6.67, carbohydrate_g 18.67) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a chicken ham sandwich assembly; no zero-macro justification required. trans_fat_g 0.07 is a confirmed trace value consistent with processed poultry products per USDA SR Legacy. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it does not constitute an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source instead.
